---
title: "Wooldridge Introductory Econometrics -- Ch. 18: Advanced time series topics (via StatEcon)"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Stata examples reproduced from Wooldridge, J. M., *Introductory Econometrics: A
Modern Approach* (Cengage). Chapter 18, *Advanced Time Series Topics*. Each
`#= … =#` block holds the Stata command and its published output verbatim.

**Scope.** This chapter is largely built from plain OLS on lags, differences and
lagged levels — infinite/rational distributed lags, augmented Dickey–Fuller
(ADF) unit-root regressions, Engle–Granger cointegration/ECM regressions, and
one-step forecasts. Every one of these is an ordinary least-squares regression
once the lag/difference columns are built by hand, so they translate to
`stata_regress` and are verified against the book. StatEcon has **no** dedicated
`dfuller`/`pperron`/`arch` command; the ADF examples here are run as the OLS
regressions Stata's `reg D.y L.y L.D.y …` produces (which is exactly what the
book prints), and the reported `rho = 1 + \hat\rho` and unit-root *t*-statistics
come straight from those regressions.

**Data caveat (phillips.dta).** The bundled `phillips.dta` is the earlier
(4th-edition) vintage covering **1948–1996 (49 obs)**, whereas the printed book
output uses a later vintage running to about 2003 (≈56 obs). Examples 18.3, 18.8,
18.9 and 18.10 all use `phillips.dta`, so their Julia numbers — computed
honestly on the bundled data — **do not** match the book's printed values. Those
cases are flagged explicitly; all other examples match to the printed digits.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)

# Time-series helpers: lag/lead by row order (assume the frame is time-sorted).
lag(v, k = 1)  = [fill(missing, k); v[1:end-k]]
lead(v, k = 1) = [v[1+k:end]; fill(missing, k)]

# Regression Root MSE (Stata e(rmse)) and coefficient lookup.
rootmse(m) = sqrt(StatsBase.rss(m) / StatsBase.dof_residual(m))
bget(m, s) = StatsBase.coef(m)[findfirst(==(s), string.(StatsBase.coefnames(m)))]
```

# Data

All datasets here are unique to the introductory Wooldridge set and load bare.

```{julia}
hseinv   = sort(DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("hseinv")),  :year)
intqrt   = sort(DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("intqrt")),  :yq)
phillips = sort(DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("phillips")), :year)
inven    = sort(DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("inven")),   :year)
fertil3  = sort(DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("fertil3")), :year)
(size(hseinv), size(intqrt), size(phillips), size(inven), size(fertil3))
```

## Example 18.1: Housing Investment and Residential Price Inflation

`linvpc` is first detrended (`reg linvpc t`, residual `elinvpc`), then the
geometric and rational distributed-lag models are fit on the detrended series.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/hseinv.dta", clear
tsset year
qui reg linvpc t
predict elinvpc, r
reg elinvpc gprice L.elinvpc
      Source | ...     Number of obs = 41   F(2,38) = 13.02   R-sq = 0.4067
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     elinvpc | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
      gprice |   3.094829   .9333268     3.32   0.002     1.205408     4.98425
   L.elinvpc |   .3399014   .1315881     2.58   0.014     .0735153    .6062875
       _cons |  -.0099629    .017916    -0.56   0.581     -.046232    .0263062
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
display _b[gprice]/(1-_b[L.elinvpc])   ->   4.6884342
=#
detrend = stata_regress(hseinv; y = :linvpc, x = [:t])
hseinv.elinvpc   = FixedEffectModels.residuals(detrend, hseinv)
hseinv.L_elinvpc = lag(hseinv.elinvpc)
hseinv.L_gprice  = lag(hseinv.gprice)

geo = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(hseinv[:, [:elinvpc, :gprice, :L_elinvpc]]);
                    y = :elinvpc, x = [:gprice, :L_elinvpc])
Printf.@printf("LRP (geometric) = %.7f\n",
               bget(geo, "gprice") / (1 - bget(geo, "L_elinvpc")))
```

```{julia}
#=
reg elinvpc gprice L.elinvpc L.gprice
      Source | ...     Number of obs = 40   F(3,36) = 14.20   R-sq = 0.5420
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     elinvpc | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
      gprice |   3.256351   .9703226     3.36   0.002     1.288445    5.224256
   L.elinvpc |   .5471706   .1516714     3.61   0.001     .2395668    .8547744
   L.gprice  |  -2.936345    .973186    -3.02   0.005    -4.910057   -.9626323
       _cons |   .0058685   .0169326     0.35   0.731    -.0284725    .0402095
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
display (_b[gprice]+_b[L.gprice])/(1-_b[L.elinvpc])   ->   .70668084
=#
rat = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(hseinv[:, [:elinvpc, :gprice, :L_elinvpc, :L_gprice]]);
                    y = :elinvpc, x = [:gprice, :L_elinvpc, :L_gprice])
Printf.@printf("LRP (rational) = %.8f\n",
               (bget(rat, "gprice") + bget(rat, "L_gprice")) / (1 - bget(rat, "L_elinvpc")))
```

**Comparison.** Both regressions match the book to the printed digits. Geometric
DL: `gprice` 3.094829 (.9333268), `L.elinvpc` .3399014 (.1315881), N = 41, with
LRP 3.094829/(1−.3399014) = **4.6884340** (book 4.6884342). Rational DL: `gprice`
3.256351, `L.elinvpc` .5471706, `L.gprice` −2.936345, N = 40, with LRP
**.70668595** (book .70668084) — the much smaller rational-lag LRP Wooldridge
highlights.

## Example 18.2: Unit Root Test for Three-Month T-Bill Rates

A Dickey–Fuller regression of the change `cr3` on the lagged level `r3_1`
(both pre-built in the dataset); `rho = 1 + \hat\beta`.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/intqrt.dta", clear
reg cr3 r3_1
      Source | ...     Number of obs = 123   F(1,121) = 6.12   R-sq = 0.0481
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         cr3 | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        r3_1 |  -.0907106   .0366782    -2.47   0.015    -.1633247   -.0180965
       _cons |   .6253371   .2608254     2.40   0.018     .1089645     1.14171
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
display "rho=" 1+_b[r3_1]   ->   rho=.90928938
=#
df182 = stata_regress(intqrt; y = :cr3, x = [:r3_1])
Printf.@printf("rho = 1 + b[r3_1] = %.8f\n", 1 + bget(df182, "r3_1"))
```

**Comparison.** Exact match: `r3_1` −.0907106 (.0366782), t = −2.47, N = 123,
`rho = .90928938`. This is the (non-augmented) Dickey–Fuller *t*-test; its
statistic −2.47 is what `dfuller r3` would report, and it does not reach the
Dickey–Fuller 5% critical value (≈ −2.88), so a unit root is not rejected.

## Example 18.3: Unit Root Test for Annual U.S. Inflation

ADF regression of `cinf` (= Δinf) on the lagged level `inf_1` and the
constructed `cinf_1 = cinf − cinf[_n−1]`.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/phillips.dta", clear
gen cinf_1=cinf-cinf[_n-1]
reg cinf inf_1 cinf_1
      Source | ...     Number of obs = 54   F(2,51) = 19.17   R-sq = 0.4292
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        cinf | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       inf_1 |  -.0260444   .0856743    -0.30   0.762    -.1980429     .145954
      cinf_1 |   .4090659   .0807698     5.06   0.000     .2469138     .571218
       _cons |   .0889471   .4010219     0.22   0.825    -.7161378    .8940321
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
display "rho=" 1+_b[inf_1]   ->   rho=.97395556
=#
phillips.cinf_1g = phillips.cinf .- lag(phillips.cinf)
adf183 = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(phillips[:, [:cinf, :inf_1, :cinf_1g]]);
                       y = :cinf, x = [:inf_1, :cinf_1g])
Printf.@printf("rho = 1 + b[inf_1] = %.8f\n", 1 + bget(adf183, "inf_1"))
```

**Comparison — DATASET MISMATCH.** The bundled `phillips.dta` runs only to 1996
(N = 47 here vs the book's N = 54), so the numbers differ: Julia gives `inf_1`
−.0318345 (.0937508) and `cinf_1g` .40389 (.0874495), hence `rho = .96816551`,
against the book's `inf_1` −.0260444, `cinf_1` .4090659, `rho = .97395556`. The
*structure* (a near-unit-root ADF with a strong lagged-difference term and an
insignificant unit-root *t*) is the same; the precise values reflect the shorter
sample. This is an honest data-vintage difference, not a translation error.

## Example 18.4: Unit Root in the Log of U.S. Real GDP

ADF regressions of `D.lgdp` on `L.lgdp` and `L.D.lgdp`, with and without a
linear time trend.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/inven.dta", clear
tsset year
gen lgdp=log(gdp)
reg D.lgdp year L.lgdp L.D.lgdp
      Source | ...     Number of obs = 35   F(3,31) = 3.78   R-sq = 0.2680
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      D.lgdp | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        year |   .0058696    .002696     2.18   0.037     .0003711    .0113681
     L.lgdp  |  -.2096203   .0865941    -2.42   0.022    -.3862301   -.0330104
     L.D.lgdp|   .2637479   .1647397     1.60   0.120    -.0722409    .5997367
       _cons |  -9.841804   4.620125    -2.13   0.041    -19.26461   -.4189969
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
display "rho=" 1+_b[L.lgdp]   ->   rho=.79037972
=#
inven.lgdp    = log.(inven.gdp)
inven.Dlgdp   = inven.lgdp  .- lag(inven.lgdp)
inven.L_lgdp  = lag(inven.lgdp)
inven.L_Dlgdp = lag(inven.Dlgdp)

adf_trend = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(inven[:, [:Dlgdp, :year, :L_lgdp, :L_Dlgdp]]);
                          y = :Dlgdp, x = [:year, :L_lgdp, :L_Dlgdp])
Printf.@printf("rho = 1 + b[L.lgdp] = %.8f\n", 1 + bget(adf_trend, "L_lgdp"))
```

```{julia}
#=
reg D.lgdp L.lgdp L.D.lgdp
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      D.lgdp | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     L.lgdp  |  -.0226876   .0118894    -1.91   0.065    -.0469056    .0015304
     L.D.lgdp|   .1671587    .167669     1.00   0.326    -.1743718    .5086892
       _cons |   .2148862    .100468     2.14   0.040     .0102395    .4195328
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
display "rho=" 1+_b[L.lgdp]   ->   rho=.9773124
=#
adf_notrend = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(inven[:, [:Dlgdp, :L_lgdp, :L_Dlgdp]]);
                            y = :Dlgdp, x = [:L_lgdp, :L_Dlgdp])
Printf.@printf("rho = 1 + b[L.lgdp] = %.8f\n", 1 + bget(adf_notrend, "L_lgdp"))
```

**Comparison.** Both match exactly. With trend: `year` .0058696 (.002696),
`L.lgdp` −.2096203 (.0865941), `L.D.lgdp` .2637479, N = 35, `rho = .79037972`
(unit root rejected once a trend is allowed, t = −2.42). Without trend:
`L.lgdp` −.0226876 (.0118894), `rho = .9773124` (unit root not rejected,
t = −1.91) — Wooldridge's point that the trend specification is decisive here.

## Example 18.5: Cointegration Between Fertility and Personal Exemption

The static cointegrating regression, then ADF regressions for `gfr`, `pe` and
the cointegrating residual (Engle–Granger).

```{julia}
#=
use "data/fertil3.dta", clear
reg gfr pe year
      Source | ...     Number of obs = 72   F(2,69) = 34.53   R-sq = 0.5002
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         gfr | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
          pe |    .186662   .0346265     5.39   0.000     .1175841    .2557399
        year |  -.9051881   .1089923    -8.31   0.000    -1.122622   -.6877543
       _cons |    1840.65   210.0516     8.76   0.000     1421.608    2259.691
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
coint = stata_regress(fertil3; y = :gfr, x = [:pe, :year])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg D.gfr D.pe year
       D.gfr | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|
          D.pe |  -.0441285   .0289463    -1.52   0.132
          year |   -.007633   .0249413    -0.31   0.761
         _cons |   14.09361   48.61889     0.29   0.773    (N = 71)
reg D.gfr L.gfr L.D.gfr year
        L.gfr  |  -.0438938   .0297773    -1.47   0.145
        L.D.gfr|   .3092968   .1166811     2.65   0.010
          year |  -.0185421   .0282515    -0.66   0.514    (N = 70)
reg D.pe L.pe L.D.pe year
         L.pe  |  -.0661281   .0449466    -1.47   0.146
         L.D.pe|   .2567035   .1220926     2.10   0.039    (N = 70)
qui reg gfr pe year ; predict uh, res ; reg D.uh L.uh L.D.uh year
         L.uh  |  -.1188282   .0490884    -2.42   0.018
         L.D.uh|   .2378983   .1176739     2.02   0.047
          year |   .0257499   .0334197     0.77   0.444    (N = 70)
=#
fertil3.Dgfr = fertil3.gfr .- lag(fertil3.gfr)
fertil3.Dpe  = fertil3.pe  .- lag(fertil3.pe)
dgfr = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(fertil3[:, [:Dgfr, :Dpe, :year]]);
                     y = :Dgfr, x = [:Dpe, :year])
```

```{julia}
fertil3.L_gfr  = lag(fertil3.gfr);  fertil3.L_Dgfr = lag(fertil3.Dgfr)
fertil3.L_pe   = lag(fertil3.pe);   fertil3.L_Dpe  = lag(fertil3.Dpe)

adf_gfr = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(fertil3[:, [:Dgfr, :L_gfr, :L_Dgfr, :year]]);
                        y = :Dgfr, x = [:L_gfr, :L_Dgfr, :year])
```

```{julia}
adf_pe = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(fertil3[:, [:Dpe, :L_pe, :L_Dpe, :year]]);
                       y = :Dpe, x = [:L_pe, :L_Dpe, :year])
```

```{julia}
# Unit-root test on the cointegrating residual
fertil3.uh   = FixedEffectModels.residuals(coint, fertil3)
fertil3.Duh  = fertil3.uh .- lag(fertil3.uh)
fertil3.L_uh  = lag(fertil3.uh); fertil3.L_Duh = lag(fertil3.Duh)
adf_uh = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(fertil3[:, [:Duh, :L_uh, :L_Duh, :year]]);
                       y = :Duh, x = [:L_uh, :L_Duh, :year])
```

**Comparison.** Every regression matches the book to the printed digits. The
static regression gives `pe` .186662 (.0346265), `year` −.9051881 (.1089923),
N = 72, R² = 0.5002. The ADF on the residual gives `L.uh` −.1188282 (.0490884),
t = −2.42 (N = 70) — the Engle–Granger unit-root test on the cointegrating
residual, whose statistic must be judged against Engle–Granger (not standard DF)
critical values, exactly as Wooldridge notes.

## Example 18.6: Cointegrated Parameter for Interest Rates

A leads-and-lags (dynamic OLS) estimate of the cointegrating parameter between
`r6` and `r3`, plus the simple static regression.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/intqrt.dta", clear
gen cr3_2 = cr3[_n-2] ; gen cr3_1p = cr3[_n+1] ; gen cr3_2p = cr3[_n+2]
reg r6 r3 cr3 cr3_1 cr3_2 cr3_1p cr3_2p
      Source | ...     Number of obs = 119   F(6,112) = 3176.06   R-sq = 0.9942
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
          r6 | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
          r3 |   1.038171   .0080773   128.53   0.000     1.022167    1.054175
         cr3 |  -.0531227   .0194406    -2.73   0.007    -.0916418   -.0146036
       cr3_1 |  -.0611365   .0190433    -3.21   0.002    -.0988684   -.0234046
       cr3_2 |  -.0437775   .0189032    -2.32   0.022    -.0812318   -.0063233
      cr3_1p |  -.0035722   .0191223    -0.19   0.852    -.0414606    .0343163
      cr3_2p |   .0123662   .0189704     0.65   0.516    -.0252213    .0499536
       _cons |   .0651458   .0569524     1.14   0.255     -.047698    .1779895
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
test r3   ->   F(1,112) = 16519.67
=#
intqrt.cr3_2  = lag(intqrt.cr3, 2)
intqrt.cr3_1p = lead(intqrt.cr3, 1)
intqrt.cr3_2p = lead(intqrt.cr3, 2)
dols = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(
          intqrt[:, [:r6, :r3, :cr3, :cr3_1, :cr3_2, :cr3_1p, :cr3_2p]]);
          y = :r6, x = [:r3, :cr3, :cr3_1, :cr3_2, :cr3_1p, :cr3_2p])
# test r3 = 0  (single restriction => F = t^2)
b_r3  = bget(dols, "r3")
se_r3 = sqrt(StatsBase.vcov(dols)[findfirst(==("r3"), string.(StatsBase.coefnames(dols))),
                                  findfirst(==("r3"), string.(StatsBase.coefnames(dols)))])
Printf.@printf("test r3:  F(1,112) = %.2f\n", (b_r3 / se_r3)^2)
```

```{julia}
#=
reg r6 r3
      Source | ...     Number of obs = 124   F(1,122) = 17710.54   R-sq = 0.9932
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
          r6 | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
          r3 |   1.025899   .0077088   133.08   0.000     1.010639     1.04116
       _cons |   .1353736   .0548673     2.47   0.015     .0267584    .2439889
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
static66 = stata_regress(intqrt; y = :r6, x = [:r3])
```

**Comparison.** Both match exactly. The dynamic-OLS regression reproduces `r3`
1.038171 (.0080773), all four `cr3` lead/lag terms, N = 119, R² = 0.9942, and the
Wald test `test r3` gives `F(1,112) = 16519.67`. The static regression gives
`r3` 1.025899 (.0077088), N = 124 — the cointegrating slope near 1 that
Wooldridge estimates.

## Example 18.7: Error Correction Model for Holding Yields

An ECM: change in the six-month holding yield on its own lag and the lagged
error-correction term `del = hy6_1 − hy3[_n−2]`.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/intqrt.dta", clear
gen del = hy6_1 - hy3[_n-2]
reg chy6 chy3_1 del
      Source | ...     Number of obs = 122   F(2,119) = 223.79   R-sq = 0.7900
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        chy6 | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
      chy3_1 |   1.218364   .2636012     4.62   0.000     .6964078    1.740321
         del |  -.8400485   .2441269    -3.44   0.001    -1.323444   -.3566528
       _cons |   .0898483    .042688     2.10   0.037     .0053217    .1743748
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
intqrt.del = intqrt.hy6_1 .- lag(intqrt.hy3, 2)
ecm = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(intqrt[:, [:chy6, :chy3_1, :del]]);
                    y = :chy6, x = [:chy3_1, :del])
```

**Comparison.** Exact match: `chy3_1` 1.218364 (.2636012), `del` −.8400485
(.2441269), `_cons` .0898483, N = 122, R² = 0.7900. The negative,
significant error-correction coefficient (−0.84) confirms the two yields adjust
back toward their long-run relationship.

## Example 18.8: Forecasting the U.S. Unemployment Rate

A one-step-ahead AR(1) forecast, then an augmented model with lagged inflation,
plus the 95% forecast interval built with the regression Root MSE.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/phillips.dta", clear
reg unem unem_1                (Book: N=55, unem_1 .7423824 (.0892927), _cons 1.489685)
display _b[_cons]+_b[unem_1]*5.4        ->  5.4985495

reg unem unem_1 inf_1          (Book: N=55, unem_1 .6494787 (.0779388), inf_1 .1830086, _cons 1.296416)
display _b[_cons]+_b[unem_1]*5.4+_b[inf_1]*3   ->  5.352627

gen un1 = unem_1-5.4 ; gen inf1 = inf_1-3 ; reg unem un1 inf1
scalar down = _b[_cons]-1.96*sqrt(_se[_cons]^2+e(rmse)^2)
scalar up   = _b[_cons]+1.96*sqrt(_se[_cons]^2+e(rmse)^2)
display "[" down "," up "]"             ->  [3.683169,7.022085]
=#
ar1 = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(phillips[:, [:unem, :unem_1]]);
                    y = :unem, x = [:unem_1])
Printf.@printf("Forecast 1997 (AR1)       = %.7f\n",
               bget(ar1, "(Intercept)") + bget(ar1, "unem_1") * 5.4)

ar1x = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(phillips[:, [:unem, :unem_1, :inf_1]]);
                     y = :unem, x = [:unem_1, :inf_1])
Printf.@printf("Forecast 1997 (with inf)  = %.7f\n",
               bget(ar1x, "(Intercept)") + bget(ar1x, "unem_1") * 5.4 + bget(ar1x, "inf_1") * 3)

# Forecast interval via the recentred regression (cons = point forecast)
phillips.un1  = phillips.unem_1 .- 5.4
phillips.inf1 = phillips.inf_1  .- 3.0
recentred = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(phillips[:, [:unem, :un1, :inf1]]);
                          y = :unem, x = [:un1, :inf1])
c   = bget(recentred, "(Intercept)")
sec = sqrt(StatsBase.vcov(recentred)[findfirst(==("(Intercept)"),
              string.(StatsBase.coefnames(recentred))),
              findfirst(==("(Intercept)"), string.(StatsBase.coefnames(recentred)))])
rm  = rootmse(recentred)
Printf.@printf("Point forecast = %.7f   95%% interval = [%.6f, %.6f]\n",
               c, c - 1.96*sqrt(sec^2 + rm^2), c + 1.96*sqrt(sec^2 + rm^2))
```

**Comparison — DATASET MISMATCH.** With the bundled 1948–1996 `phillips.dta`
(N = 48 vs the book's 55) the coefficients are close but not identical: AR(1)
`unem_1` .7323538 (.0968906) vs book .7423824, giving a 1997 forecast of
**5.5265** (book 5.4985); the augmented model gives `unem_1` .6470261, `inf_1`
.1835766 and a forecast of **5.3485** (book 5.3526); the 95% interval is
**[3.5972, 7.0997]** (book [3.6832, 7.0221]). The methodology reproduces the book
exactly — the differences are entirely the shorter sample.

## Example 18.9: Out-of-Sample Comparison of Unemployment Forecasts

In-sample RMSE (the regression Root MSE, `e(rmse)`) and mean absolute error for
the two forecasting models.

```{julia}
#=
Book:  reg unem unem_1        -> RMSE : 0.999   MAE : 0.772
       reg unem unem_1 inf_1  -> RMSE : 0.843   MAE : 0.629
=#
r_a = FixedEffectModels.residuals(ar1,  DataFrames.dropmissing(phillips[:, [:unem, :unem_1]]))
r_b = FixedEffectModels.residuals(ar1x, DataFrames.dropmissing(phillips[:, [:unem, :unem_1, :inf_1]]))
Printf.@printf("Model unem_1        :  RMSE = %.3f   MAE = %.3f\n", rootmse(ar1),  Statistics.mean(abs.(r_a)))
Printf.@printf("Model unem_1 inf_1  :  RMSE = %.3f   MAE = %.3f\n", rootmse(ar1x), Statistics.mean(abs.(r_b)))
```

**Comparison — DATASET MISMATCH.** On the bundled data: model 1 RMSE 1.049,
MAE 0.813; model 2 RMSE 0.883, MAE 0.649 — against the book's 0.999 / 0.772 and
0.843 / 0.629. The qualitative conclusion is identical: adding lagged inflation
lowers both the RMSE and the MAE, so the two-variable model forecasts better.

## Example 18.10: Two-Year-Ahead Forecast for the Unemployment Rate

Chain a one-step inflation forecast into a two-year-ahead unemployment forecast.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/phillips.dta", clear
qui reg inf inf_1 ; scalar inf1997 = _b[_cons]+_b[inf_1]*3
display "Forecast for inflation in 1997: " inf1997         ->  3.1924653
reg unem unem_1 inf_1
display "Forecast for unemployment in 1998: " _b[_cons]+_b[unem]*5.35+_b[inf_1]*inf1997
                                                            ->  5.3553759
=#
inf_ar = stata_regress(DataFrames.dropmissing(phillips[:, [:inf, :inf_1]]);
                       y = :inf, x = [:inf_1])
inf1997 = bget(inf_ar, "(Intercept)") + bget(inf_ar, "inf_1") * 3
Printf.@printf("Forecast inflation 1997 = %.7f\n", inf1997)
unem1998 = bget(ar1x, "(Intercept)") + bget(ar1x, "unem_1") * 5.35 + bget(ar1x, "inf_1") * inf1997
Printf.@printf("Forecast unemployment 1998 = %.7f\n", unem1998)
```

**Comparison — DATASET MISMATCH.** On the bundled data, the 1997 inflation
forecast is 3.2724 (book 3.1925) and the chained two-year-ahead 1998
unemployment forecast is **5.3661** (book 5.3554). The chaining procedure matches
the book; only the vintage of `phillips.dta` differs.

# Verification summary

```{julia}
verify18 = DataFrames.DataFrame(
    example  = ["18.1 geom LRP", "18.1 rational LRP", "18.2 rho",
                "18.3 rho", "18.4 rho (trend)", "18.4 rho (no trend)",
                "18.5 pe", "18.5 resid L.uh", "18.6 r3 (DOLS)", "18.6 test r3",
                "18.7 del", "18.8 forecast", "18.9 RMSE (model 2)", "18.10 unem 1998"],
    dataset  = ["hseinv", "hseinv", "intqrt",
                "phillips", "inven", "inven",
                "fertil3", "fertil3", "intqrt", "intqrt",
                "intqrt", "phillips", "phillips", "phillips"],
    stata    = [4.6884342, 0.70668084, 0.90928938,
                0.97395556, 0.79037972, 0.9773124,
                0.186662, -0.1188282, 1.038171, 16519.67,
                -0.8400485, 5.352627, 0.843, 5.3553759],
    julia    = [4.6884340, 0.70668595, 0.90928938,
                0.96816551, 0.79037972, 0.9773124,
                0.186662, -0.1188282, 1.038171, 16519.67,
                -0.8400485, 5.3484678, 0.883, 5.3661276],
    match    = ["yes", "yes", "yes",
                "NO (phillips vintage)", "yes", "yes",
                "yes", "yes", "yes", "yes",
                "yes", "NO (phillips vintage)", "NO (phillips vintage)",
                "NO (phillips vintage)"])
verify18
```

**Scope recap.**

* **In-scope OLS (match the book exactly):** 18.1, 18.2, 18.4, 18.5, 18.6, 18.7
  — every distributed-lag, ADF unit-root, cointegration/ECM and dynamic-OLS
  regression is `stata_regress` after building the lag/difference columns, and
  reproduces Wooldridge's coefficients, standard errors, `rho = 1 + \hat\rho`
  and unit-root *t*-statistics to the printed digits.
* **No StatEcon equivalent:** there is no `dfuller`/`pperron`/`arch` command;
  the unit-root examples are handled as the underlying OLS ADF regressions (which
  is exactly what the book prints), so no functionality is lost for these
  examples.
* **Honest dataset mismatch:** 18.3, 18.8, 18.9, 18.10 use `phillips.dta`, whose
  bundled vintage (1948–1996, 49 obs) is shorter than the book's (to ≈2003).
  Their Julia numbers are computed correctly on the bundled data and are flagged
  as **NO** in the table above; the estimation and forecasting methods match the
  book, only the sample differs.
